text:
WHOLE-BODY FDG PET/CT SCAN

EXAMINATION: Whole-body fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) positron emission tomography with concurrent low-dose non-contrast CT (PET/CT), skull base to mid-thigh.

INDICATION: 66-year-old female with newly diagnosed pancreatic head mass, biopsy-confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Prior multiphasic CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis demonstrated a hypoenhancing 3.8 x 3.2 cm mass in the pancreatic head and uncinate process with extensive contact with the superior mesenteric artery and involvement of the superior mesenteric vein. CA 19-9 markedly elevated at 1,247 U/mL at time of diagnosis. PET/CT performed for comprehensive metabolic staging and to evaluate for occult distant metastatic disease prior to initiation of planned neoadjuvant systemic chemotherapy.

TECHNIQUE: The patient fasted for greater than 6 hours prior to tracer administration. Serum glucose at time of injection was 134 mg/dL. The patient received 10.8 mCi (approximately 400 MBq) of F-18 FDG intravenously. Images were acquired approximately 62 minutes post-injection. Whole-body PET emission data were acquired in 3D mode and iteratively reconstructed with attenuation correction using the concurrent low-dose CT. The co-registered low-dose CT was obtained without intravenous or oral contrast and is used primarily for attenuation correction and anatomic localization; it is not a diagnostic-quality contrast-enhanced CT and should be interpreted in conjunction with the dedicated pancreatic protocol CT performed at this institution.

COMPARISON: Multiphasic CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (pancreatic protocol), performed at this institution.

---

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK:
No FDG-avid cervical, supraclavicular, or submandibular lymphadenopathy. No hypermetabolic lesions in the visualized brain parenchyma. No abnormal metabolic activity in the thyroid gland or salivary glands. Physiologic symmetric FDG activity is noted in the bilateral tonsils and palatine region.

CHEST:
Lungs: No discrete pulmonary nodules with associated FDG uptake. The lungs appear clear on low-dose CT with no consolidation, effusion, or masses identified. No endobronchial lesions. No FDG-avid mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. The mediastinum demonstrates normal physiologic activity in the blood pool without focal hypermetabolic lymph nodes. No pleural effusion or pleural-based FDG avidity. Cardiac activity is physiologically elevated; no pericardial effusion identified on CT correlation.

UPPER ABDOMEN:

Pancreas: There is a markedly FDG-avid mass centered in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas. The lesion demonstrates intense, heterogeneous tracer uptake with a measured peak SUVmax of 8.9 (mean SUV approximately 5.4). On co-registered CT, this corresponds to the known hypoenhancing mass measuring approximately 3.8 cm in greatest dimension, consistent with the primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma as characterized on the recent dedicated contrast-enhanced CT. The intense metabolic activity is clearly delineated from background pancreatic parenchyma, which shows markedly reduced to absent FDG uptake consistent with parenchymal atrophy and upstream ductal obstruction. No hypermetabolic satellite foci within the pancreatic body or tail are identified.

Regional lymph nodes: A single focus of moderately increased FDG uptake is identified in the peripancreatic/periportal region, anterior to the portal confluence, corresponding to a 1.2 cm lymph node seen on the dedicated contrast CT. SUVmax measures 4.2. This level of activity is clearly above background soft tissue uptake and is suspicious for nodal metastatic involvement. No other regional lymph nodes along the superior mesenteric artery distribution, celiac axis, para-aortic chain, or portocaval region demonstrate abnormal FDG avidity. No FDG-avid aortocaval lymphadenopathy.

Liver: The liver demonstrates homogeneous background FDG activity consistent with normal hepatic parenchyma. No discrete hepatic lesion or focal area of hypermetabolism is identified to suggest hepatic metastasis. No biliary ductal dilatation is apparent on the low-dose CT; however, the previously described mild intrahepatic biliary dilation and common bile duct diameter of 12 mm noted on the diagnostic contrast CT are acknowledged. No hepatic parenchymal lesions are identified on CT correlation.

Gallbladder: Normal in appearance. No FDG avidity.

Spleen: Homogeneous splenic FDG activity, within the normal physiologic range. No focal lesions.

Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands demonstrate no focal FDG uptake above background. No adrenal masses on CT correlation.

Stomach: Mild physiologic FDG activity in the gastric wall, within expected limits. No abnormal mural thickening or mass lesion.

Small and large bowel: Normal physiologic FDG activity throughout the visualized bowel without focal hypermetabolic lesions suspicious for mucosal neoplasm or serosal implants.

PERITONEUM AND MESENTERY:
No peritoneal implants, serosal deposits, or focal areas of FDG hypermetabolism along the peritoneal surfaces, mesentery, or omentum to suggest carcinomatosis. No ascites identified on low-dose CT.

RETROPERITONEUM:
No para-aortic or paracaval lymph nodes with increased FDG activity. No retroperitoneal masses. The aorta and major vessels are unremarkable on CT.

PELVIS:
No FDG-avid pelvic lymphadenopathy. No osseous lesions in the visualized pelvis. Uterus and ovaries: The uterus appears normal in size with no focal FDG uptake. Physiologic renal collecting system and bladder FDG activity without focal lesions.

MUSCULOSKELETAL / OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:
No focal osseous FDG-avid lesions in the visualized axial or appendicular skeleton to suggest skeletal metastatic disease. No lytic or sclerotic foci on CT correlation suspicious for osseous involvement. Degenerative changes are noted in the thoracolumbar spine consistent with the patient's age, without associated hypermetabolic activity.

SOFT TISSUES:
No abnormal FDG-avid soft tissue masses or subcutaneous lesions identified.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. INTENSELY FDG-AVID PRIMARY PANCREATIC MASS: Markedly hypermetabolic mass in the pancreatic head and uncinate process (SUVmax 8.9), entirely consistent with the biopsy-confirmed pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. The degree of metabolic activity reflects an aggressive, highly glycolytic tumor in keeping with the known pathologic diagnosis.

2. FDG-AVID REGIONAL LYMPH NODE: A single peripancreatic/periportal lymph node demonstrates moderately increased FDG uptake (SUVmax 4.2), consistent with nodal involvement and concordant with findings on prior contrast CT. No additional nodal stations demonstrate pathologic metabolic activity.

3. NO HEPATIC METASTASES: No FDG-avid hepatic lesions identified. The liver appears metabolically and morphologically normal on this examination.

4. NO PULMONARY METASTASES: No hypermetabolic pulmonary nodules or masses. The lungs appear clear.

5. NO PERITONEAL DISEASE: No evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis or serosal implants by metabolic or CT criteria.

6. NO DISTANT ORGAN OR SKELETAL METASTASES: No hypermetabolic foci in the adrenal glands, bones, or other distant sites.

OVERALL IMPRESSION: FDG PET/CT is consistent with locoregional pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. The dominant FDG-avid primary tumor in the pancreatic head (SUVmax 8.9) with a single suspicious peripancreatic lymph node (SUVmax 4.2) represents regional disease without detectable distant metastatic spread. The absence of occult hepatic, peritoneal, or pulmonary disease on this functional imaging study is clinically significant in the context of treatment planning. These findings support proceeding with neoadjuvant systemic therapy as the next management step, consistent with the clinical staging of borderline resectable, locoregional pancreatic adenocarcinoma.

NOTE: Mildly elevated serum glucose at time of FDG injection (134 mg/dL) in the setting of known diabetes mellitus may modestly limit sensitivity for small metastatic foci; however, overall image quality is deemed adequate for staging purposes.
